Metric: Software name? (string)
URL? (URL)
Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Bullet / PyBullet: Bullet Physics open-source community (originally by Erwin Coumans)
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Coppelia Robotics AG (Zurich, Switzerland); originally developed within Toshiba R&D
MuJoCo: Google DeepMind (acquired October 2021); originally developed at University of Washington Movement Control Lab by Emanuel Todorov; commercialized by Roboti LLC
OpenRAVE: Carnegie Mellon University Robotics Institute / Quality of Life Technology Center (originated 2006); later maintained at JSK Robotics Lab, University of Tokyo; primary maintainer: Rosen Diankov (MUJIN Inc.)
PhysX (open-source SDK): NVIDIA Corporation; originally developed by NovodeX AG (Switzerland); acquired by Ageia Technologies in 2004; acquired by NVIDIA in 2008
AirSim: Microsoft Research (developed at Microsoft AI & Research); original team led by Shital Shah, Debadeepta Dey, and Ashish Kapoor
Drake: Toyota Research Institute (TRI); MIT CSAIL / Robot Locomotion Group (origin)
DART: Georgia Institute of Technology, Graphics Lab and Humanoid Robotics Lab (originated 2011, created by C. Karen Liu and Mike Stilman); University of Washington Personal Robotics Lab; Open Source Robotics Foundation; Stanford University Movement Lab; Carnegie Mellon University
CARLA: Computer Vision Center (CVC), Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona / Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC), Barcelona; Intel Labs; Embodied AI Foundation; collaborative open-source community
MORSE: LAAS-CNRS (Laboratoire d'Analyse et d'Architecture des Systèmes, French National Center for Scientific Research), Toulouse, France; jointly developed with ONERA (French aerospace lab); contributions from 15+ universities worldwide
Simbody: Simbios, NIH National Center for Physics-Based Simulation of Biological Structures at Stanford University; primary developers Michael Sherman and Peter Eastman (Stanford Simbios Center); Scott Delp Neuromuscular Biomechanics Lab (Stanford)
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Kavraki Lab, Rice University (lead development); Dr. Lydia Kavraki (PI), Dr. Mark Moll, Dr. Zachary Kingston, Dr. Ioan Șucan
Pinocchio: Inria (French National Institute for Research in Digital Science and Technology), Paris; LAAS-CNRS (Laboratoire d'Analyse et d'Architecture des Systèmes, Toulouse); primary developer: Justin Carpentier (Inria Willow team); co-creator: Nicolas Mansard (LAAS-CNRS Gepetto team)
OROCOS: KU Leuven / PMA Division, Belgium; founded by Herman Bruyninckx (KU Leuven, December 2000); initial implementation by Peter Soetens (PhD student KU Leuven); copyright shared by 20+ contributors internationally
SOFA: Inria (France); four active Inria teams: Mimesis, Imagine, Defrost, Asclepios; founded 2006 by Inria alongside USTL, UJF, CNRS, and MGH (Massachusetts General Hospital)
MoveIt!: PickNik Robotics (PickNik Inc.) leads development; originally developed by Sachin Chitta and Dave Coleman at Willow Garage / SRI International; maintained by broad ROS community
GraspIt!: Columbia University Robotics Group (Professor Peter Allen, lab of origin); current maintainers: Matei Ciocarlie (Columbia Robotics & Rehabilitation Lab), Jake Varley (Google), Jon Weisz; historical contributors at Columbia and Carnegie Mellon
Habitat: Meta AI Research (FAIR — Fundamental AI Research); developed by Facebook AI Research; AI Habitat project part of FAIR's embodied AI research program led by researchers including Erik Wijmans, Abhishek Kadian, Amir Sadeghi, Claudia Perez-D'Arpino, and many others
Project Chrono: University of Wisconsin-Madison (primary lead; Dan Negrut, Simulation-Based Engineering Lab) and University of Parma, Italy (co-developer; Alessandro Tasora); supported by NSF, US Army, and other funders
Flightmare: Robotics and Perception Group (RPG), University of Zurich and ETH Zurich; lead developers: Yunlong Song (ETH Zurich RPG), Selim Nusbaum, and team; PI: Davide Scaramuzza (ETH Zurich RPG director)
MRPT: University of Almería (Spain); lead developer: Jose Luis Blanco-Claraco (Full Professor at University of Almería, formerly TU Munich and University of Málaga); founded 2005 by Jose Luis Blanco
Newton Dynamics: Independent open-source project; primary developer: Julio Jerez; co-developer: Alain Suero (original co-author); community contributions via GitHub
YARP: Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia (IIT), Genoa, Italy; RobotCub Consortium (EU-funded iCub humanoid robot project); primary developers: Lorenzo Natale, Daniele Pagnin, and IIT Robotics team; originates from humanoid robot research (iCub)
OpenRTM-aist: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Japan; primary developer: Noriaki Ando (AIST Intelligent Systems Research Institute); OpenRTM-aist Project team at AIST

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Gazebo: Open-source robotics simulator providing high-fidelity physics, rendering, and sensor models for robot simulation and testing
Webots: Open-source multi-platform robot simulator for modeling, programming, and simulating robots, vehicles, and mechanical systems
Bullet / PyBullet: Python module for physics simulation, robotics, and deep reinforcement learning based on Bullet Physics SDK
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Versatile and scalable robot simulation framework for rapid algorithm development, factory automation simulation, fast prototyping, verification, and digital twin creation
MuJoCo: General-purpose physics simulator for research and development in robotics, biomechanics, graphics and animation, and machine learning requiring fast and accurate simulation of articulated structures
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): Open-source, high-performance library for simulating rigid body dynamics; used in robotics, games, and interactive simulation applications
ROS 2: Open-source software development kit (SDK) and middleware framework for robotics: provides communication infrastructure (DDS-based pub/sub), hardware abstraction, drivers, libraries, visualizers, tools, and community packages for building robot applications
OpenRAVE: Open-source robotics planning framework for testing, developing, and deploying motion planning algorithms; focuses on simulation and analysis of kinematic and geometric information for robot motion planning, especially manipulation
PhysX (open-source SDK): PhysX is a high-performance real-time physics engine SDK for simulating rigid bodies, particles, vehicles, deformables, and related physical effects; primarily used in video games and other real-time simulation applications.
AirSim: Open-source simulator for autonomous vehicles (drones/quadrotors and ground vehicles) built on Unreal Engine; supports testing AI systems, computer vision, deep learning, and reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicle research; provides photorealistic environments, physics simulation, and sensor models (cameras, LiDAR, IMU)
Drake: Open-source C++ and Python toolbox for model-based design and verification of robotics systems; focuses on multibody dynamics, trajectory optimization, model predictive control, perception, manipulation planning, and formal verification for robustly safe robots
DART: Open-source C++ physics engine and kinematics/dynamics library for robotics and computer animation; provides kinematic and dynamic simulation of articulated structures with an emphasis on accurate and stable simulation for manipulation and locomotion research
CARLA: Open-source autonomous driving simulator built on Unreal Engine; provides photorealistic urban environments, accurate physics, and a comprehensive sensor suite (cameras, LiDAR, radar, IMU, GPS) for developing, training, and validating autonomous driving systems and AI agents
MORSE: Generic academic robotic simulator based on the Blender 3D engine and its integrated Bullet physics engine; provides a component-based architecture for simulating robots and their interactions with environments, emphasizing middleware-agnostic design (ROS, YARP, Pocolibs, etc.)
Simbody: High-performance open-source C++ multibody dynamics library for simulating articulated biomechanical and mechanical systems; provides accurate physics simulation of skeletons, robots, and vehicles; serves as the physics engine underlying OpenSim (biomedical simulation software)
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Open-source C++ library for sampling-based motion planning algorithms; provides implementations of planners such as PRM, RRT, RRT*, EST, and many others; designed as a framework for motion planning research and integration into robotics software (e.g., MoveIt, OpenRAVE)
Pinocchio: Fast and flexible C++ library for rigid-body dynamics computations and their analytical derivatives; implements state-of-the-art algorithms (RNEA, CRBA, ABA, JSIM) for computing kinematics, dynamics, and Jacobians of articulated rigid-body models; includes Python bindings and supports robotics, animation, and optimal control applications
OROCOS: Open-source C++ framework for real-time robot control; major components: Real-Time Toolkit (RTT) for component-based hard real-time software; Kinematics and Dynamics Library (KDL) for serial chain kinematics; Bayesian Filtering Library (BFL); Orocos Component Library (OCL)
SOFA: Open-source real-time medical simulation and interactive simulation framework; provides component-based architecture for deformable object simulation, finite element methods, collision detection, and haptic feedback; primary applications in surgical simulation, medical education, and soft robotics
MoveIt!: Open-source robotics manipulation framework for ROS and ROS 2; provides motion planning, kinematics, collision checking, trajectory execution, and perception integration for robot arms and manipulators; integrates OMPL, CHOMP, STOMP, and other planners as plugins
GraspIt!: Simulation environment for robotic grasping research; supports loading robot hand models and 3D object models to plan, evaluate, and visualize grasps; implements grasp quality metrics (epsilon and volume quality measures), contact model simulation, and the GraspIt! eigengrasp planning framework; integrates Columbia Grasp Database (CGDB)
Habitat: Flexible, high-performance 3D simulator for embodied AI research; enables training and evaluation of embodied AI agents (virtual robots, navigation agents) in photorealistic 3D environments; provides efficient 3D scene rendering (Magnum renderer), dataset support (Matterport3D, Gibson, Replica, HM3D), and physics simulation via Bullet; companion to Habitat-Lab RL training framework
Project Chrono: High-performance open-source multi-physics simulation library; provides multibody dynamics, finite element analysis (FEA), rigid and flexible body simulation, vehicle dynamics (Chrono::Vehicle), granular terrain (Chrono::Granular), fluid-solid interaction (Chrono::FSI), and sensor simulation (Chrono::Sensor); supports GPU-accelerated simulation via CUDA
Flightmare: Flexible modular quadrotor simulator for learning and control research; provides rendering-based environment (Unity3D rendering engine) and physics simulation for training reinforcement learning agents on drone racing and agile flight tasks; modular architecture separates rendering (Unity) from RL environment (Python/C++) for flexibility in fast or photorealistic simulation modes
MRPT: Open-source cross-platform C++ library for mobile robotics research and development; provides algorithms for SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), computer vision, motion planning, and sensor data processing; includes a 3D robotics simulation environment (RawLogViewer, SceneViewer3D); supports mapping, localization, path planning, and sensor fusion
Newton Dynamics: High-performance physics simulation engine for games and interactive applications; provides rigid body dynamics, collision detection, soft bodies, vehicle dynamics, and articulated rigid bodies; optimized for real-time deterministic simulation with exact arithmetic; provides Newtonian dynamics solver with an exact implicit solver for rigid body integrations; widely used in game development and robotics simulation
YARP: Open-source middleware for humanoid and general robotics; provides communication infrastructure for distributed robot systems (ports, carriers, protocols); supports inter-process communication between robot sensor and actuator modules; includes libraries for sensor processing, motor control, computer vision, and device abstraction; primary middleware for the iCub humanoid robot platform and used in many academic humanoid robot projects
OpenRTM-aist: Robotic Technology Middleware (RTM) implementation for the OMG-standardized RT (Robot Technology) Component standard; provides a component-based framework for creating, managing and connecting RT-Components (robot software modules); enables distributed robot systems by abstracting hardware and software interfaces into standardized components; includes component framework, execution context management, CORBA-based inter-component communication, and development tools (RTCBuilder, RTSystemEditor); widely used in Japanese robotics industry and research
